subject: Raj Rajaratnam and the Galleon Group: how the FBI used court-authorized wiretaps — a tool built for the mob — to catch the most powerful insider-trading ring on Wall Street, and played the traders their own voices
central_question: For decades, insider trading was treated as a victimless ghost — everyone suspected the game was rigged, but no one could ever prove who whispered what to whom. So what happens the first time the FBI stops trying to read the suits' minds and simply records their phone calls — and plays a billionaire his own voice?
viewer_promise: By the end, the viewer understands how the top of Wall Street was quietly rigged by inside whispers, how a single wiretap turned suspicion into proof, why agents borrowed the mob playbook to take down a $7 billion hedge fund, and why this case is the moment the system finally caught one of its own — on tape.
surprise: The shock is not that a hedge-fund billionaire cheated. It is the method: for the first time, federal agents wiretapped Wall Street suits the same way they bug mobsters and drug cartels — and the men who thought they were untouchable convicted themselves, in their own words, because a recorded voice does not forget.
stakes: When the edge stops being research and becomes a private telephone line into the boardroom, the market everyone else trusts is quietly stolen. The question under the whole case: can a system this rich and this connected ever actually catch the people at the very top — or only the ones too small to matter?
target_audience: English-speaking US general audience interested in true crime, Wall Street, money, fraud, the FBI, and how powerful people finally get caught
demand_evidence: Insider trading + a $7B hedge fund + the FBI using mob-style wiretaps is a proven high-CTR true-crime/finance hook; the case ('the Galleon case', the wiretaps, the perp walk) is widely reported and still cited as a landmark.
A clean, satisfying takedown of a Wall Street billionaire — caught on his own tape — delivers the rare 'the system finally caught one' payoff that drives likes, comments, and completion.
competition: Heavily reported at the time (Reuters, NYT, WSJ, Bloomberg) and dramatized in finance media, but few retellings make the WIRETAP itself the protagonist — the cinematic core of agents hearing the crime in the defendants' own voices — or land it as a single 30-minute auteur arc that pays off one opening question.
differentiation: A 30-minute single-arc film built around one device: the recorded voice. Movements move from the gold of the trading floor, to the cold blue of the surveillance room, to the verdict's flat daylight. Second-person pull ('you'), the 'voice on the wire' motif, a clean moral payoff (vindication, not schadenfreude). No real-person likeness; all imagery symbolic and brand-consistent.
research_feasibility: High: a federal jury convicted Rajaratnam at trial (verdict May 11, 2011); the record is public via DOJ/SDNY press releases, SEC litigation releases, the FBI's own account, and the Second Circuit opinion upholding the wiretaps (US v. Rajaratnam, 2013), plus extensive major-outlet reporting.
visual_feasibility: High via symbolic AI imagery + the factory finance/data/surveillance shelf: a glowing trading floor going silent, an audio waveform freezing into evidence, a reel-to-reel turning in a dark room, a network of lit phone lines across a city skyline, an empty courtroom, gavel light. No real-person likeness (invariant 11); no real logos/seals.
risk_notes: R2 — Raj Rajaratnam is living and was CONVICTED AT A JURY TRIAL (verdict May 11, 2011, 14 counts). His conviction and sentence are stateable as fact; describe the underlying conduct as what 'the jury found' / 'prosecutors proved' rather than free editorializing.

Do NOT conflate co-defendants. Rajat Gupta was a SEPARATE defendant, convicted June 2012, sentenced to 2 years + a $5M fine — never merge Gupta's conduct or penalties into Rajaratnam's. Tippers (Goel, Kumar, Moffat, Chiesi) were charged separately; state each precisely.

Profit is scope-dependent (SEC >$25M then >$52M; broader probe figures higher; prosecutors 'over $60M'). SAFEST on screen: the court forfeiture '$53.8 million' or 'more than $50 million' — never a single flat invented number.
Attribute the superlative 'longest insider-trading sentence in U.S. history (at the time)' to prosecutors/press, not as a free-standing eternal fact.

No real-person likeness or deepfake of Rajaratnam or any associate, juror, agent, or judge (invariant 11). All AI visuals symbolic and disclosed; no real corporate logos, court seals, or identifiable real locations.
kill_conditions: Cannot keep co-defendants cleanly separated (esp. Gupta) through legal review; or the core figures (verdict date/counts, 11-year sentence, forfeiture $53.8M + $10M fine, SEC $92.8M penalty, wiretaps-upheld) cannot be re-verified to primary DOJ/SEC/court records at publish time; or the auteur structure cannot hold 30-min retention without sensationalizing.
